questions.prompt: What transfer function is used in the single pole and zero example?
questions.explanation: The second example in the note studies H(s)=(s+C)/(s+B) with positive B and C.

questions.prompt: What additional condition is stated in the note for this example?
questions.explanation: The note specifically analyzes the case 0<C<B, which places the zero closer to the origin than the pole.

questions.prompt: After substituting s=j omega, what is H(omega)?
questions.explanation: Replacing s by j omega gives the frequency response H(omega)=(j omega + C)/(j omega + B).

questions.prompt: What is |H(omega)| for this pole-zero example?
questions.explanation: The magnitude is the magnitude of the numerator divided by the magnitude of the denominator, giving the ratio of the two square roots.

questions.prompt: What is the phase response for this example?
questions.explanation: The phase is the angle of the numerator minus the angle of the denominator, so the note writes tan^-1(omega/C)-tan^-1(omega/B).
Substituting omega=0 leaves the real ratio C/B, which is below one because C<B.

questions.prompt: What is the high-frequency magnitude limit?
questions.explanation: At large omega, both numerator and denominator are dominated by omega, so their ratio tends to one.

questions.prompt: Why is the phase positive when 0<C<B?
options: Because omega/C is always larger than omega/B, so the first inverse tangent is larger
Because the pole angle is always positive
Because the magnitude is below one
Because the numerator has no imaginary part
questions.correctAnswer: Because omega/C is always larger than omega/B, so the first inverse tangent is larger
questions.explanation: The note points out that omega/C exceeds omega/B, so tan^-1(omega/C) is larger than tan^-1(omega/B), making the phase positive.

questions.prompt: What happens to the phase at very large frequency?
questions.explanation: Both inverse tangent terms tend to 90 degrees as omega becomes large, so their difference tends back to zero.

questions.prompt: How does the note describe H(s)=(s+1)/(s+2)?
options: It resembles a high-pass filter, though the cutoff is not sharp
It is an ideal low-pass filter
questions.correctAnswer: It resembles a high-pass filter, though the cutoff is not sharp
questions.explanation: The note uses cautious language and says the characteristic resembles a high-pass filter, but the 3 dB cutoff is not sharp.
